column4: A brand as a "touchpoint" to help post collegiate athletes fine community.
column6: I appreciated that you broke down the elements of how college athletics differs from normal athletes. It felt like a natural transition to move into the stressors. You smartly zeroed in on the brand elements of your tool.
column8: Really well paced and clear, your presentation moved elegantly and I appreciated the clever animation elements.
column10: Excellently paced. Be mindful of "ums."
column12: Really thoughtful development of your visuals; you chose mockups that nicely elaborated on your presentations. The swimcap was great! If we can get the app up and running that would be amazing.
column14: You held everyone's attention really well here.
column16: HQ's notes on the nav-bar are really well delivered. The idea of selling this app for a Run Club is really thoughtful — I like the idea of extending this into real world elaborations. Smart feedback on the "on-boarding" process from Stefan. Reach out to Sarah to meet that friend to help them off-board Olympic Athletes. What does it look like when the photos users take are not great!
column17: Well done; I feel like the translation of this app into reality will be a great space to test this app — basically I want you to take your front-end elements into Cursor. Watch the green/yellow for accessibility. A secondary color would be awesome. Fouc

column4: A branding project for a set of whole foods.
column6: I feel like breaking down the nutrition labels was super helpful. I could imagine a bit more time on this specific topic. I do think you're a bit behind, but nothing that you can't make up.
column8: Well-paced. I'd love to see a social media presence.
column10: Clear and coherent.
column12: I feel like we're missing quite a lot right now, and it will require a bit of a push here. We should see how these elements sit with your brand on the fruit. Mockups will make these especially legible.
column16: Really great notes from Stefan — how do we see your narrative integrated into the form. Good note on a "Rooted" score. Customer relationships and brand — how can you allow for two user journeys.
column17: Make it real! I'd love to see a sticker sheet.

column4: A tool to help new travelers.
column6: Excellent graphic showing us the variety of places you traveled. Well done anticipating the expansiveness of the travel market. I feel like this all elegantly built up to your solution. Well done bring us to your interview.
column8: Clear and easy transitions.
column10: Very clear, but be mindful of the back of the room.
column12: You brand typeface is working really well (I also love that monospace type here). I could imagine you using a pre-existing brand library to develop this further.
column16: HQ: Not thinking differently enough. It's a solid suggestion to treat this as an assistant. What does the Matka-branded assistant look-like. Robinhood's on-boarding experience.  World Currency as a graphic suggestion. Use Framer. Small AI models.
column17: I love the watercolor, but I could imagine this being tricky to read at an app scale.

column4: A personal digital archive for memorizable items.
column6: I love the shift from your personal story to the product. Super smart to highlight the heirlooms and ephemera. Aunt Jane's note pad works elegantly.
column8: Really great transitions here. Naturally shifts. People also love specific, personal stories.
column10: Could be a bit louder for the back.
column12: Really solid excision here. Beware of accessibility of colors! I would love to see you artfully tag these elements as well.
column16: Stefan makes a.great note to have this app show random items. How can you create narrative? Sarah makes a great point about search-ability? Riley's note about the playing with the quilt motif is a wise one. Transcribe. Own the language of "Family Thread."
column17: You might check out the Creative Independent for tagging and highlighting. The secondary interaction will emerge in code.

column4: Designing an intentional "third space."
column6: Your graphic language throughout is in a really solid state. I feel like your introduction to the project is the strongest.
column8: Nicely paced work throughout.
column10: Clear,  at points the description was a bit muddled.
column12: In general, really solid graphics but watch your type on the lists of text (an opportunity for buttons). Definitely make the guy blink.
column16: HQ's challenge is a solid one: what is the micro-prototype that could work as location first. This is a natural trajectory for building a third space. A quick prototype: Brick as a model — digital flyering.

column4: A publication solely art and design on campus.
column6: You nicely brought us into your publication experience. You clearly laid out your model, and I appreciate the clarity of your discussion. I'd love to see a diagram of how you laid out your work.
column8: Love the small marginalia touches to transition us to a new piece.
column10: Be mindful of the back row.
column12: Diagram this process for me. Consider a web component to develop their own books: bindery.js.
column16: Stefan's note about "Now" and "Next" — two different angles and products. What makes this unique from "The Phoenix?"
column17: Consider your sleeve as a brandable move.

column4: A program and a strategy to celebrate children artists.

column10: Very clear!
column12: I feel like there's a possibility to build with animation.
column16: HQ: noted wisely that social media gives you your museum. You could partner with museums. Sarah: some roundness... Gallery 37 Chicago.
column17: I'm still not quite convinced by the dotted lines.

column4: A virtual reality
column6: Your humor was really thoughtfully deployed, and it was elegantly balanced with your design. Well done breaking down your science (and smart deployment of your way-finding metaphors here). You might put up some of the "Why VR" rebuttals on your screen.
column8: Really great to see you aware of the audience's expectations.
column10: Your tone and pacing was excellent! You were a good model for Clayla.
column12: I could imagine your logomark animating when we first jump into SomaMorph.
column14: The audience was really with you, but just be mindful of some your clarification moments.
column16: HQ: What could be your "Soma" self? Is there a personalization element in the game? Maybe you can add to your experience? Stefan: If it's not about leveling up, how do you invite people to come back? Lead with your results; "this is how you should expect to feel after investing time with Clayla" Riley: positioning it as an appointment. The shut down of the Metaverse should be clarified as "Lessons learned."

column4: A gift-box service for service members to utilize for their family.
column6: The personal story works really well, and it's a naturally transition into your research. The pop-up information was useful. Well scripted!
column8: Excellent movement from concept to process.
column10: Very clear and distinctive.
column12: Love the animation, and smart development of your type here. People were nodding along to this animation moment. I was only confused a bit on the SMS messaging slide.
column14: Really great anticipation of your audience's attention span. Great listening face!
column16: You really thoughtfully answered the audience questions. Great work here. "Sponsoring boxes" is a lovely gesture here. Sarah: perhaps there's a "personal" something that you can add to the box (HQ) to encourage "attunement."
column17: I wonder if you could point to the "stress points" in the presentation itself.

column4: Open design tools
column6: Smart to introduce the "daunting" element of this process. The journey through bitmap is really successful. Artful move to a specific break down of your design tools.
column8: You really did a great job pacing. The process slides are elegant.
column10: Perhaps a bit louder for the people in the back.
column12: I appreciate that you kept your slides fairly simple. The animation was really thoughtful.
column16: HQ: The point that HQ made about Haiku. Get instant feedback from Threads.Stefan: look into the philosophy behind unix.

column4: A program to bring access across geographical proximity.
column6: The infographics are super useful; such a beautiful spelling out of "geographical privilege." I could imagine a diagram elaborating on how your process would work.
column8: The transitions work, but I could imagine you breaking down the program in a section.
column10: A bit mumbly at points, but the script is really strong.

column10: Very clear.
column12: I feel like the visuals are working. currently I could see those illustrations as buttons? The breakdown of your website is super effective here. I don't think that the type is especially visible.
column16: Tom: good job explaining your authority on the topic. The type is far too small for legibility. Ditch the clip art, and instead bring in your illustration earlier.

column4: An adobe-killing tool.
column6: I think you need to ground your research in quantitative data ("a lot" of people). You should be showing me how it works (definitely record your screen here). I need to see it in action. The revision to the file-manager is exciting, I need to see it in action. Animate how you got to your logomark.
column8: Clear transitions.
column10: Clear and well-paced.
column12: I think you need to make more distinctive choices on your typefaces in the presentation. It feels very default-y. You need another secondary typeface in your brand (Pragmatica extended might need a regular version).
column16: Tom: you have to establish what you aim to do here. What can you get done with "less" — pull in something like "Procreate." Piper: what do these tools afford people to do? Stephanie: Ground this too for a specific person.

Smart to put your work at the forefront. "Struggling with Place." (Maybe rotate those papers). I appreciate how you shared the roads not taken. Pivoted thesis is a good term — "I want to know what people are doing?" I feel like highlighting Odell and Denzer as means grounds this nicely. Smart to end on the tapestry as a natural form, as a natural response! 

I think this is a great development of the project!
